An alternative solution is to store hashes of all the cached files here and then leave the original cache files as-is.

This would be more complex though and would still mean the files could get out of sync though the code here could be changed to tell if so. body:
I've been thinking about this more just now and I think this goes beyond what we've discussed in the past. I'm wondering if when running `deno fmt` or `deno lint` should we have `deno.json` files in sub directories be resepected when there's a `deno.json` in an ancestor directory.

For example, say you have:

```
sub_dir
  - deno.json
  - file.ts
deno.json
````

1. Should running `deno fmt` have the same behaviour on `file.ts` as running `deno fmt sub_dir/file.ts`?
2. Should running `deno fmt sub_dir/file.ts` have the same behaviour as running `(cd sub_dir && deno fmt file.ts)`?

I can think of a few options:

**Option 1: Resolve based on closest deno.json from file path**

This option would mean for `deno fmt` and `deno lint` along with `deno fmt <file-path>`, it would always resolve a config file based on the closest deno.json. So includes/excludes in parent `deno.json`s would be ignored.

* Fmt/lint option resolution: Each config file resolves from the default options
* Includes/excludes: Each config file resolves from the default options
* Run options: Resolved from closest ancestor *deno.json* (Same as Option 3)

Personally, I think this is the best option and is the least complex for users to understand. It answers yes to both the questions I asked above.

**Option 2: Resolve using resolution from all deno.json files in ancestor directories from file path**

This would require resolving all deno.json files in all ancestor directories, then use that information to figure out the fmt/lint options and includes/excludes for a folder based on the combined result of all config files.

* Fmt/lint option resolution: Each config file resolves from the ancestor directory's options
* Includes/excludes: Each config file resolves from the ancestor directory's includes/excludes and overrides it.
* Run options: Requires resolution of each ancestor config and then a resolution to the final options by combining configs.

This would be complex to implement. It answers yes to both the questions I asked above.

**Option 3: Use config file resolved for root of each disjointed tree**

By "disjointed tree" I mean, directory sub trees that share no common directories from the root with other directory trees. Maybe there is a better word?

This would resolve the config file for each disjointed tree. So `deno fmt` would resolve based on the cwd and use that, but `deno fmt ./child/mod.ts ../sibling/mod.ts ../sibling/other.ts` would resolve the config file for `./child` and `../sibling` to use for their respective files.

* Fmt/lint option resolution: Each config file resolves from the root of each disjointed tree
* Includes/excludes: Each config file resolves from the root of each disjointed tree
* Run options: Resolved from closest ancestor *deno.json* (same as Option 1)

I think this is the solution we agreed upon before? It answers no to both of the questions I asked above.
author: dsherret

body:
Option 1 and Option 3 are the same except applying to multiple arguments. I agree with this approach.

Or we can just find the deno.json based on the first argument and if other files have a different config, erroniously ignore it for the time being. These are corner cases that we should have tests for anyway - we can move to option 1/3 in the future.
author: ry

body: I believe Option 3 was exactly what we discussed previously.

body: I've just checked, and `Vec::with_capacity` calls the allocator without guaranteeing that the memory has been zeroed. Sure, the allocation might've actually been reused, or it might be a new allocation that actually has been zeroed, but even if we could guarantee that it's either of those cases, I'm pretty sure the language treats the result of an allocation as uninitialized memory, which means that creating values from it, even `u8` values, would be undefined behavior.

body:
We have up to 3 publishes that occur when a change is made:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/eda6e58520276786bd87e411d0284eb56d9686a6/cli/lsp/diagnostics.rs#L605-L612

When each of the three categories finish being calculated, they publish.  This code is only measuring from change to first publish, not full publish.  I am not sure what you are wanting here.

I realised when looking at the code again, we are debouncing "all" the diagnostics, when in fact we only care about debouncing the "typescript" ones because of their blocking nature for other API calls, which degraded performance:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/eda6e58520276786bd87e411d0284eb56d9686a6/cli/lsp/diagnostics.rs#L172-L184

We could/should break that out, so that the "lint" and "deno (dependency)" diagnostics are calculated independently and not debounced.  There might be a little bit work to do, because we treat the dirtyness of the diagnostics per document, and what we are saying is that lint and deno diagnostics are one version of dirty and typescript is another version of dirty.

body:
That is trying to be tested here is that the diagnostics for dependents get invalidated and recalculated.

For example if you have `a.ts` the exports something which is imported into `b.ts`, when you modify `a.ts`, it impacts that diagnostics of `b.ts` without `b.ts` being edited.  We had issues where diagnostics were hanging around until you actually touched/modified a dependent file.

body: It's a little strange that we now have `exists` and `contains_specifier`, which both do the same thing. I don't think we need two methods. What's the performance impact of using `contains_specifier` vs `exist`? When TypeScript does an `exists` call it will then do a second call load the file, so is it a big deal to just load it when doing `.contains_specifier()` as it will just be loaded right after?

body: I moved the code from `exists` here, but I still think we should still just do `self.get(specifier).is_some()` because it's simpler and uses the same code path as `.get` so it future proofs this method and ensures the code paths act the same. Also, in tsc when it calls `exists`, if true then it will cause `.get` to be called so there's not really any reason to not just load it the first time in my opinion (maybe doing an exists check is faster than getting the last modified time that `get` does though?). That said, I don't have strong opinions on it and this extra code isn't too bad to have.

body:
I think this specifier metadata and #12864 are not really document related, but rather have to do with specifiers. Also, `FileSystemDocuments` contains only non-open documents.

Maybe we should move this out to a separate struct on its own and then I think we'd only need to use that struct in diagnostics.rs? (So we could remove most of the code in this file)
author: dsherret

body:
I disagree... they are very much "FileSystemDocuments" as they are what is stored in the cache... while we don't load 100% of files in the cache as document nor do 100% of documents that are loaded get their meta data loaded, they both are representations of information in the cache and have a coupled shared state.

Documents open in the editor for editing can't have metadata, only `FileSystemDocuments` can (and only those in the cache).
author: kitsonk

body:
I think they should share the same underlying cache, but we should separate this out from `FileSystemDocuments`. The purpose of `FileSystemDocuments` is to prevent having multiple copies of the same `Document` loaded into memory.

I would also say this shouldn't be on the interface for `Documents` either. We're wanting to know what the metadata is for certain specifiers and not documents. For example, there could be two specifiers that point at the same document and only one of those specifiers would produce a certain diagnostic. Again, they would share the same underlying cache.

Maybe I'm misunderstanding the purpose of this though.

body:
This change highlights that the cache being disconnected from the data in the `Arc<Mutex<_>>` is a little sketch for the sake of the snapshots, but this state being in sync is currently enforced by the "lsp mutex". In the future, if we allow multiple threads in, this state being in sync should be further enforced by requiring mutability on `set_location`, so we're good.

We may consider some improvements to this in the future to ensure the two are forced to stay in sync when a snapshot occurs so the code is slightly more resilient to changes around it, but this could be a very minor future improvement.

body: Callbacks that run when exiting Deno.
author: littledivy

body:
Actually in node.js, I don't think it is called when exiting the main process, rather it is only called when child worker threads exit. Node loads the target NAPI library once, and if/when child threads try to load the same NAPI library, the same library (loaded symbols) is reused, and the module initialization method is called (again) for that worker thread. So `napi_add_env_cleanup_hook` exists so that when a worker thread exits, the NAPI code can clean up resources from that worker and still continue to properly operate with the remaining worker threads.

And yeah, I was a little surprised by this behavior as well :).
author: kriszyp

body:
Yeah okay, the variant cannot be removed nor can it be made into a zero-size one nor a `()` one.

The fields are used by `serde_v8` to determine which variant it will actually serialize to, but the FFI Rust code does not know that serde will be interested in the fields.

Another option would be to filter out all of the static declarations on JS side and only dispatch the load for the functions. That would make the whole enum unnecessary.

body:
This is better, but I think still not exactly correct if I'm understanding the code correctly. I'm not sure we should be counting all ops, but instead only counting ops that weren't started when the test ran.

Example:

1. User starts Test A without sanitizers. It finishes with 1 pending op and Test B starts.
2. While Test B is running, the op from Test A completes.
3. Test B completes with 1 pending op.
4. Sanitizers incorrectly pass because the diff is 0.

I guess this is an existing issue though and maybe something we should fix in a separate PR.

body: The chosen approach is actually intentional: the "enabled" flag is a boolean (a primitive), so assigning it to `Deno.core` assigns it by value, not by reference. To fix this, we'd need to change the check in `opAsync` to be `if (core.opCallTracingEnabled)` instead of `if (opCallTracingEnabled)`. The former introduces an additional property lookup to all `opAsync` calls which I don't really want as it is a super hot path.

body:
# mvp
- support for up to nanoseconds accuracy and formatting
- output should also contain min/max, can be displayed as `avg unit/op (min..max unit/op)` or we can take inspiration from [criterion.rs](https://github.com/bheisler/criterion.rs) [BenchmarkDotNet](https://github.com/dotnet/BenchmarkDotNet) [hyperfine](https://github.com/sharkdp/hyperfine) ![](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/684911491035430919/945533883921875034/unknown.png)

# optional/post mvp
- warmup before measuring (10-1000 iterations or 1s worth of iterations with minimum of 4-10 iterations)
- `time` option to limit benchmark to time quota
- print hardware specs (see BenchmarkDotNet)
- benchmarks grouping / baseline to compare to (see hyperfine)
- compare results with previous run (see criterion)

body:
I kind of already know this to be a not-good-idea but:

`v8::String` can (if externalizable) be turned into `v8::String::ExternalStringResource` which can be used to to get to get the actual string buffer data. Now that buffer can be used to create a `v8::BackingStore` and then sent onwards to a `v8::ArrayBuffer`. => Zero copy encoding.

Of course, this means that the AB now owns the string buffer and I'm very much unsure if the original `v8::String` can deal with that, much less what happens if the AB now makes changes to the buffer data.

@bartlomieju this is ok to me. But I think this is due to the different way the `std::net` treats the presence of 
a socket in the **TIME_WAIT** state compared to `socket2`. During the test I used the `netstat` utility to investigate why these test failures appened, and I noticed that the socket previously closed by other test were in the **TIME_WAIT** state, thus not allowing other sockets to bind to their hostname-port pair.

body:
Yes, but this is a limitation of how steps are declared and the impact is "minimal", in the sense that this only comes into play when:

- The step was not statically discovered
- The step with the same name at the same depth within the same top level test exists

The outcome would be that the runtime identified step would not have its result reported against it, but instead the earlier duplicate.

To fix this, we need globally unique IDs to come from the harness for tests and steps, which is outside of the scope of this PR.

We don't provide location info for thrown values which aren't `Error` objects. Browsers usually fall back to the throw site in these situations, or in this case the `reportError()` call site. That's what the tests are expecting us to do. Of course that's trivial for browsers which implement `reportError()` as a direct native binding, since `v8::Exception::create_message(scope, exception)` would "just work". But for us we need to manually analyse the stack trace within `reportError()`.

body:
@bartlomieju and me have looked into this PR. The implementation is good. We are a little concerned about that this PR adds the `onerror` event handling from [report the exception](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/#report-the-exception) but only for the `reportError` codepath. There are some other codepaths which are meant to use this "report the exception" handling (for example timers and event targets), but we currently don't do that. I think that is rather confusing.

I think we may want to split this out into two PRs: one that adds the infrastructure for "report an exception" and wires it up to timers and event targets, and then a second PR that actually adds `reportError`, built on top of the "report an exception" infrastructure. WDYT?

There is a bit of a possibility for false negative here: Even if there are '=' characters at the end it does not mean that they'll be removed below since that depends on whitespace removal etc.

As such, it would be more appropriate to track '=' markers at end with an enum: None, One, Two. Then compare to those when '=' is being removed and reset the enum appropriately. If reset does not happen, it means that the '=' at the end were invalid characters.

body: **SO_REUSEADDR** defaults to true since, as stated in #13808, the previous implementation of TCP socket used the [std::net](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/6343edfa11c80ffed02de7e059178d4c1637ecdb/library/std/src/sys_common/net.rs) implementation, which sets **SO_REUSEADDR** to true when binding a **TcpListener** outside of Windows. In this way it is possible to keep the behavior of existing code that does not explicit use the new **reuseAddress** argument. Setting this to **false** as default value could break existing code due to the fact that in Unix system is not possible to bind socket to a combination of address+port occupied by closed sockets in the **TIME_WAIT** state, as visible in failed tests in #13794. In addition, this seem the same behavior of node: [unix tcp](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/e46c680bf2b211bbd52cf959ca17ee98c7f657f5/deps/uv/src/unix/tcp.c), [win tcp](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/e46c680bf2b211bbd52cf959ca17ee98c7f657f5/deps/uv/src/win/tcp.c).

body:
We don't leak it, `tc` is essentially a transmute that works with our TypeId dispatch.

`x` is a low-level copy of `src`, so in the case of strings we get two `String`s structs that actually point to the same buffer which violates single ownership principles so we forget the first without dropping it since otherwise that will lead to double-free and use-after-free problems.

body: Not actually fine. The safety requirements for `Vec::set_len` include making sure that the elements `0..len` are initialized, which they're not in this case. https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.set_len has a similar sample where the FFI method is first passed `vec.as_mut_ptr()`, and the length is changed afterwards.
author: andreubotella

body: @andreubotella It is initialized right after this call through the call to V8 (it writes to the buffer). The guarantee holds.
author: lucacasonato

author: littledivy

body: My understanding is that they have to be initialized at the time of the call.
author: andreubotella

body: @andreubotella What we are doing here is essentially the FFI example from the standard library docs, except that the "ffi" call (the rust call to v8) takes a `vec` that it then turns back into `(pointer, len)`, rather than taking a `(pointer, len)` directly.
author: lucacasonato

body: The relevant difference is that in the std example at no point there are uninitialized elements in the vec, because `set_len` is only called after they are initialized in the FFI call.

body:
Some of my work touches on a ring buffer of sorts, where the implementation has been done such that the cursor is always traveling backwards. I don't know why that is but I have some faith in the original implementers that they didn't do it randomly but with good reason.

So, maybe give it a minute of thought if that would make sense in this case? (Though the use cases are quite different indeed, I'm definitely not saying that their way is the best. Only that there may be something there of use.)
author: aapoalas

body: Further thinking on this: The ring buffer that I referred does not have a concept of  IDs in the sense of the PromiseRing, instead it only has the "head" which keeps consuming the tail as it goes. In that scenario the inverted order probably has benefits but for Deno's use case it likely does not make sense since indexing by IDs needs to work etc.
author: aapoalas

body:
I'm not sure this is right. If a promise resolver is moved to the map, it's because a new resolver with a different id took its slot. If it is then removed from the map but the resolver that took its slot hasn't been removed yet, this method would return `true` when it shouldn't.

Also, `self.ring.get(idx)` has type `Option<&Option<PromiseResolver>>`, and the outer option is some if the index is a valid index into the slice – which it's guaranteed to be by `Self::ring_idx`. So `self.ring.get(Self::ring_idx(id)).is_some()` always returns true, even if cursor is 0.
```suggestion
    self.ring[Self::ring_idx(id)].is_some() || self.map.contains_key(&id)
```

Yes, I was going to point that out.

Internalizing struct field strings is fine since the set is finite and relatively small (for deno's ops), internalizing all Map keys gives users a footgun which can lead to unbounded memory "leaks". I would prefer this to be removed from this PR

body:
This is not a real downgrade but a downgrade of the "minimum" minor version for `1.*`. `deno` `cargo.lock` still defines 1.8.1 and will go up as needed as long as it's `< 2`.

At the same time, if we don't downgrade the minimum minor version any crate depending on `deno_core` (like the project I'm trying to use deno in) also uses `ahash` (or any of the other problematic dependencies) it won't compile as cargo won't be able solve the dependency cycle.

body: Could we refactor this to be like the other `client` calls where they call back to the client outside of the LSP mutex? This is a deadlock concern because we're holding the LSP mutex while making a call to the client, which depending on the client's implementation could make a call back to the server which then wouldn't respond to any messages until the client sent back a response to our message.
author: dsherret

body:
Here it only ever occurs once, when the client has said it has initialised.  This is exactly the point in the lifecycle where you expect to have "blocking" requests, where the server queries the client of anything that falls outside of the initialisation before handling other messages.

I believe there are two other instances, one (on config change) occurs already outside the inner mutex lock (like re-querying the specifiers).  I will double check that the other (when workspace folders are updated) follows that pattern.

This PR combines multiple changes into a single PR, making it difficult to tell what change actually causes what change. This PR has   two distinct changes: reading all parts at once via a single op, and reading them all into a single buffer with some unsafe code. author: andreubotella

body:
Here, `*const T` might be a fat pointer, but casting it to `*const u64` is enough to drop the metadata, so the intermediate cast to `*const u8` is also not needed here.

Also, the validity of this function assumes that `T` is the same size as `u64`, which doesn't seem to be guaranteed. I'm also not sure that this is sound for unsized types.

And shouldn't these opaque functions, other than `send_opaque`, be `unsafe fn`, since they impose heavy safety constraints on the callers?

No it's not, we're simply transmuting pointers/references across the "serde boundary" as opaque u64s so our specialized de/serializer can pick it up

This looks like a great feature. I have a few concerns:

1. structs-by-value are not an FFI "primitive", instead just a bunch of neatly arranged bytes.
2. This passes the ownership of the value to the ffi function. The array buffer backing memory owned by JS is always deallocated by Rust? 
4. Prior art?
5. Is it a blocker? this functionality is taken care of by the `x/byte_type` module + the `pointer ` type?

body: I guess the `self.store[self.range]` consumes the range, thus requiring the `self.range.clone()`?
author: aapoalas

body:
`.clone()` isn't always expensive, in this case it isn't (basically copying 2 usize numbers) we have to explicitly `.clone()` because `Range` doesn't impl `Copy` only `Clone`, in this `Copy` would be ~identical to `Clone` just implicit.

And https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#impl-SliceIndex%3C%5BT%5D%3E-5 expects an owned `Range<usize>`

body:
Discussed with @lucacasonato and we don't really get what's going on here. 

It feels like we should not call `printException` and just directly call `throw error`.

See:
```js
// foo.js
globalThis.addEventListener("error", (e) => {
    console.log("foo");
    e.preventDefault();
})

setTimeout(() => {
    throw new Error("boom!")
}, 1000);
```

```
❯ cargo run -- run foo.js
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.19s
     Running `target/debug/deno run foo.js`
foo2

deno on  error-event-for-events-and-timers [$!?] via  v17.4.0 via 🦀 v1.59.0 took 2s
❯ echo $status
0

```

Removing `e.preventDefault()` from the event listener yields:
```
❯ cargo run -- run foo.js
   Compiling deno_runtime v0.53.0 (/Users/ib/dev/deno/runtime)
   Compiling deno v1.20.4 (/Users/ib/dev/deno/cli)
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 23.51s
     Running `target/debug/deno run foo.js`
foo2
error: Uncaught Error: boom!
    throw new Error("boom!")
          ^
    at file:///Users/ib/dev/deno/foo.js:10:11
    at Object.action (deno:ext/web/02_timers.js:145:13)
    at handleTimerMacrotask (deno:ext/web/02_timers.js:62:12)

deno on  error-event-for-events-and-timers [$!?] via  v17.4.0 via 🦀 v1.59.0 took 26s
❯ echo $status
1

```

Please explain the reasoning for current solution
author: bartlomieju

body:
`globalThis.onerror = (e) => { ... }` is not working (but `globalThis.addEventListener("error", () => {});` is working), I think we should address it in this PR before landing.

body:
> It feels like we should not call `printException` and just directly call `throw error`.

It's so the error is printed synchronously as it is in browsers, and the actual error leading to termination is detached. I especially wanted this for `reportError()`.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> It's so the error is printed synchronously as it is in browsers

Wouldn't `throw error` also achieve this?

> and the actual error leading to termination is detached

Why is this important? It seems rather annoying to get two errors printed if something goes wrong.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> Wouldn't `throw error` also achieve this?

Without a microtask that makes it catchable around `dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent("leads-to-error"))` and `reportError()` which is undesired. The other option is throwing the main error in the microtask, should I just do that?
author: nayeemrmn

body:
Ah I see... I think we might want to tackle this differently. What about adding a core binding in the form `core.terminate(error)` that saves the error onto the isolate state, then triggers an isolate termination ([`Isolate::terminate_execution`](https://docs.rs/v8/latest/v8/struct.Isolate.html#method.terminate_execution)), which is essentially an uncatchable error. Then once this termination bubbles up to the place in `deno_core` where we'd return an error back to the Rust caller we grab the error from the isolate state and return that. Does that make sense?

This would be synchronous, and uncatchable, and would re-use existing error handling infra that `deno_core` users are used to.

body:
The JS-side options logic has somewhat changed from previous.

```ts
writeFileSync("foo", "bar", { append: "" });
```
would call `openSync("foo", { write: true, create: true, truncate: true })`, ie. append would take the `false` value.

Now the same call will lead to `core.opSync("op_write_file_sync", { path: "foo", data: "bar", append: "" })` call. I'm not sure what `serde` will do with that but on the JS side at least the logic is definitely changed.

I'm not sure if it's desired but I would've personally expected these lines to be:
```ts
append: options.append === true,
create: options.create === true,
```

> When you use the repository's GITHUB_TOKEN to perform tasks, events triggered by the GITHUB_TOKEN will not create a new workflow run. This prevents you from accidentally creating recursive workflow runs. For example, if a workflow run pushes code using the repository's GITHUB_TOKEN, a new workflow will not run even when the repository contains a workflow configured to run when push events occur.

body:
Actually, a few issues I just realized this has that need to be addressed:

a) before attempting to stream the body using the rid, we need to make sure the stream is not locked
b) we need to lock the stream while we are reading
c) we need to close the stream once we are done reading

If we don't do that last thing, the underlying resource will not be automatically closed by the `ReadableStream` implementation. This also means that `op_http_write_resource` should not close the stream resource itself.

We'll need tests for these cases.

body:
I think we should implement the `JsError::aggregate` printing in `PrettyJsError` (addressing #13831) and use that formatting here.
```suggestion
      let err_string = PrettyJsError::create(js_error)
        .to_string()
        .trim_start_matches("Uncaught ")
        .to_string();
```
Using our colourful terminal error formatting would be good for test failures. #14189 can still be done on top.

`JsError::fmt()` should remain as it is (a simple implementation around `write!(f, "{}", self.stack)`) rather than being a third formatting implementation that has to be mindful of test output.
author: nayeemrmn

body:
@nayeemrmn shouldn't we do both? What I implemented here is not really specific to tests, it's just a way to print aggregate error without multiple "Uncaught" in the middle. I agree we should implement it for `PrettyJsError`, but not implementing it for `JsError` means that the only information you'll get will be `AggregateError` if it's not formatted by `PrettyJsError` later.

body:
Did some measurements for `cli/bench/deno_common.js` and its version written using `Deno.bench`. There's still a huge discrepancy in the taken measurements (about 2-3x). Discussed with @AaronO and he suggested that we could alleviate it twofold:
a) Don't do two measurements per iteration, but instead one measurement per iter - ie. use previous iteration "end time" as a start time for next iteration. There are a bunch of math/array operation afterward, but these should be negligible compared to a syscall to get the current time
b) sample the cost of the time measurement - ie. run some loops of `benchNow` to see how much time it takes

@evanwashere do you think you could incorporate these changes in the PR (or either one of them)?

body: We have to pass both the promise and the promise ID to the JS side, so both have to be returned from the op's V8 function. Setting the `promiseIdSymbol` in the Rust side currently wouldn't work, since the promise returned by the op's V8 function isn't the same as the one returned by `Deno.core.opAsync`. But if GC pressure is a worry, we could have a single array that gets modified and returned every time.

body: Is it? Adding `default-features = false` to `v8` imports, suggests that it's not, or maybe I'm misunderstanding something?
author: bartlomieju

body:
It's a default feature of v8 https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8/pull/924, so as of today it's the default of deno core.

With that said, the only way to make it optional to users of deno core is to make it `default-features=false` at the dependency level but enable via a feature. This new feature can then be enabled by default (preserving the status quo) but can be disabled by deno core users (via `default-features=false`).

body:
I created a sample TSC project with following config:
```ts
export function hello(): string {
  return "📣❓";
}

```
```
{
    "compilerOptions": {
        "allowJs": true,
        "allowSyntheticDefaultImports": true,
        "emitDecoratorMetadata": false,
        "experimentalDecorators": true,
        "importsNotUsedAsValues": "remove",
        "incremental": true,
        "inlineSourceMap": true,
        "inlineSources": true,
        "isolatedModules": true,
        "jsx": "react",
        "module": "esnext",
        "removeComments": true,
        "sourceMap": false,
        "strict": true,
        "target": "esnext",
        "useDefineForClassFields": true,
        "useUnknownInCatchVariables": false,
    }
}
```

The output is as follows:
```js
export function hello() {
    return "📣❓";
}
//# sourceMappingURL=data:application/json;base64,
```
It does contain a proper source map that can be decoded. It looks like this is somehow caused by our implementation of TS host. I wonder if `tsc` available for node uses some kind of custom `base64` encoding function that is different than the function in default compiler host implementation (that we use).
author: bartlomieju
dsherret

body:
`deno_ffi_callback` cannot be called after the drop happens, as this drop will drop the entire `UnsafeCallbackResource`, including the C function itself owned by the `libffi::middle::Closure`. If the function pointer is still stored somewhere on the other side of the FFI border, the pointer will become invalid and trying to call the function will likely just segfault.

Well, I guess theoretically there might be the chance that the memory has not yet been reused but that seems like a stretch.

Anyway, yes the call is definitely an unsafe operation. Is the need to emphasize that beyond just documentation and the name of the whole class `UnsafeCallback` more important compared to the unified API of closing resources with a common `close()` method?

body:
ZeroCopyBuf, as its name suggests, does not copy the data from the `ArrayBuffer` but instead acts as a wrapper that can be used to reference into the memory. ZeroCopyBuf is dropped, but the `ArrayBuffer` lives until V8 decides it's time for it to be GC'd.

So, this definitely can be UB if the user passes in an AB that is then immediately dropped and a GC triggers. Otherwise it's totally fine.

author: aapoalas

body:
I am by no means an expert, but from my understanding (or lack of it) of the C ABI (or lack of it :D ) there is no way for a function call to pass actual ownership of memory.

1. Pointers are of course purely pointers to the memory. The memory itself is still owned by whoever allocated it, and the caller and callee must agree on what is found at the other end of the pointer.
2. Structs (which the FFI API currently does not actually support) pass struct parts through various registers (and others on some platforms), but these are still copies of the actual data in memory, the memory itself is still allocated and released by the same party.

Of course with structs (and even with pointers) there's a possibility that whatever is contained in the struct has pointers that should be considered to carry ownership with it. An example would be the result of Rust Box's `Box::to_raw_parts()` which returns essentially a `(*const u8, usize, usize)` struct. This struct would likely get passed in through FFI in a bunch of registers. From Deno FFI point of view this would be a `new BigIntArray(3)`. Now the question is, what happens to the three BigInts when this struct gets passed through an FFI boundary?
1. The values of the usize integers get copied to the other side of the FFI boundary.
2. The ownership of the (implied) `Box` is transferred to the other side of the FFI boundary.
3. The receiver of ownership is expected to call any required APIs when it is done with the ownership. Of course just removing the usize integers isn't going to be enough. Releasing whatever is found on the other side of pointers needs to be done according to the original allocators preferences. Generally this means calling a destructor / drop implementation.
4. The values on the original side of the FFI boundary, what happens to them? They are not changed, of course. Three (essentially) usize integers are not going to changed or deallocated. What they represent, that is the important part but it's also beyond what FFI can represent: The understanding that the first `*const u8` is a pointer referring to a contiguous memory of some length determined by the value of one of the `usize` parameters, and that whoever has last received that pointer should carry the responsibility of calling a destructor when they're done with it is plainly a contract, not one built into the ABI itself.

So, when a `TypedArray` is used as a pointer, the ownership of the memory (as a physical address space) remains always with Deno, and by extension the user of the FFI library. The user must make sure that the `TypedArray` does not go out of scope and get garbage collected for as long as the pointer is given to an FFI library. Deno's FFI can only provide the means by which the contract of memory usage is shared (passing pointers to memory), it cannot (with the current API at least) accurately represent passing of ownership.

If / once structs are supported (this is another planned followup of mine), those will likely operate on a different logic. Whereas pointers are just referring to memory, structs are generally understood to pass ownership. On a technical level the data is still copied, but eg. if a struct contains a pointer (like with the above `Box` example) then it is generally understood that the ownership is passed along with the struct. For this reason, I think the proper model for struct passing is definitely detaching ArrayBuffers.

So, where passing a `TypedArray` as pointer means that Deno must keep the buffer alive then on the other hand passing a `TypedArray` as struct would generally mean that any ownership implied by the buffer are transferred to the other side of the FFI boundary. As such, it's generally more apt that the buffer ownership is taken away from Deno and passed onto Rust (which will then deallocate it according to the ArrayBuffer's deleter callback). If then user code truly wants to retain part-ownership of the struct contained in the `TypedArray` they'll need to either plainly copy the buffer or (more likely) call some native library's copy constructor with another `TypedArray` argument into which the copy will be created.
author: aapoalas

@GJZwiers Did you check that processes actually receive the `CTR_C_EVENT`? 

According to MSDN (which is often wildly inaccurate, but still) you can only send it to `CTR_C_EVENT` to all processes in the terminal.
From: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/console/generateconsolectrlevent

> This signal cannot be limited to a specific process group. If dwProcessGroupId is nonzero, this function will succeed, but the CTRL+C signal will not be received by processes within the specified process group.

body: I was trying to write some tests for SIGINT and SIGBREAK in this PR and ran into this issue where sending a SIGINT to the child process also kills the parent in the terminal. It seems very unexpected and different than other OSes.
author: dsherret

body:
For WinOS, new "child" processes, created from a console process using `CreateProcess()`, all share the same console (*unless* created with `CREATE_NEW_CONSOLE` or `DETACHED_PROCESS`). <kbd>CTRL+C</kbd> and <kbd>CTRL+BREAK</kbd> events are sent to all processes which share that console, so all processes in the console process group will receive the event/signal.

For situations requiring shared I/O with a "parent", such as a shim, I'll just use null handlers for signals in the parent while waiting for the "child" process to finish.

ref: [MS ~ Creation of a Console](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/console/creation-of-a-console) @@ <https://archive.is/yFRT5>
ref: [MS ~ CTRL+C and CTRL+BREAK Signals](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/console/ctrl-c-and-ctrl-break-signals) @@ <https://archive.is/6T140>

body: I'm not sure it's worth focusing on optimizing `create_realm` right now, especially since the remainder of the method uses things that are only called once when setting up a runtime, and I would assume are not optimized to be on a hot path. At some point it might be important to optimize the `ShadowRealm` constructor, but I'm not sure this one change is worth it at this point.
author: andreubotella

body:
`v8::Weak<T>` is a different Rust wrapper for the C++ `v8::Global<T>`, so yeah. upgrading is much the same as cloning a global; but even if we make `results_per_realm` be `Vec<(v8::Weak<v8::Context>, ResultList)>`, the weaks would have to be cloned in order to add them to the vec.

In the end some cloning or upgrading is necessary, although with something like (ab)using V8's internal handle representation to essentially build a hashmap with `v8::Context` keys, we'd only need to clone globals for the realms that have async ops to resolve. This would be preferable, I guess.

body:
It depends on what you want to do, right? If you run only a single module, you should run the event loop to its end, even when `mod_evaluate` is already finished based, on your earlier suggestions.

So since `mod_evaluate` should always finish before `run_event_loop` and `run_event_loop` has to be run to completion even if `mod_evaluate` was done first, there wouldn't be any benefit to use `select!` here? I think for this specific simple example running it without select makes more sense, if I understood the process correctly.

body: I think this is a memory leak. You're explicitly stopping the Vec from being dropped unless you tell it to drop, and then you use `to_vec()` on it which makes a copy of it to create the Uint8Array. The copy will be properly handled and dropped by the `BackingStore` but the original will leak, I think.
author: aapoalas

body: This will return an array to async functions. The `00_ffi.js` needs to be updated to turn that array into a Uint8Array.
author: aapoalas

body: Furthermore: As long as resizable buffers are not a thing, I think the more apt type for this would be a `Box<[u8]>`.
author: aapoalas

body:
So here I think a memory leak happens. `self.structure_value` gets cloned to a new vector which is used for the `ArrayBuffer` and its lifetime is then controlled by V8. The original copy, however, is left alone and leaks.

I think the appropriate API would be `.into_inner()` from the `ManuallyDrop` type.

However, as said I think this should actually be a boxed slice. `ArrayBuffer` has a creation API for that as well.
author: aapoalas

body:
Definitely an optional thing, but it might be considered if the buffer should be detached as the struct gets "passed" to the other side of the FFI API.

Of course, there's nothing forcing us to do that, and it would make certain optimisations impossible (like using a single buffer as an out pointer and parameter both, creating from and passing by value structs to FFI without any copies). It would just be a forceful way to tell the user that structs usually move their ownership when used as parameters.

body: If it's intended, a note or renaming might be preferred (`compute_packed_struct_size`?), and I suggest test cases covering objects with padding.
author: ghost

body:
Nope. It's checking for non-zeroness. And there's a definite downside to using `backing_store[byte_offset..]`: It will panic if the backing store has a size of 0.

This is because in Rust that will mean "take the zeroth element, and all elements after that from this slice", but for a zero element vector there is no zeroth element and thus we're accessing the vector beyond its limits.
author: aapoalas

body:
Users kind of will need to know about it to use structs in Deno FFI.

eg. Imagine a `["u8", "u32"]` struct. If I'm not badly mistaken, this will essentially become `["u8", "u8" x 3 padding, "u32"]` if not packed, right? Now, if we compute the padding for the user we'll then expect an 8 byte buffer. (Side note: Users would need some way to opt out of padding for packed structs as well.)

Now, a user who does not understand padding will read `"u8"´ + `"u32"`, create a 5 byte array and pass it along. ERROR! Horrible things happen! Either we check and throw an error about the buffer being too small (good handling of this), or the receiving native library writes beyond the buffer's limits and memory gets corrupted.

Since we do not offer a way to create custom buffers from structs definition, nor do we offer any help in accessing the proper parts of it (eg. in the above case the padding bytes at index 1, 2 and 3 shouldn't really even be offered for reading), it doesn't really make sense to me to try and be automatic about padding handling either.
author: aapoalas

body:
No real opinion from myself :shrug:.
> (Side note: Users would need some way to opt out of padding for packed structs as well.)

I believe that this would make the most sense - allowing both. Nonetheless, I believe documenting the implemented behaviour is all that would suffice.
author: ghost

body:
> Nope. It's checking for non-zeroness. And there's a definite downside to using `backing_store[byte_offset..]`: It will panic if the backing store has a size of 0.

(Mentioned in the other thread about the latter, but as for non-zeroness, it would have to be `>=`/`<=`)
author: ghost

body:
`byte_offset > 0` literally says: "If byte_offset is larger than 0". ie. If there is an offset, then do slice-reference starting from that offset, otherwise just slice-reference the entire buffer.

Doing `byte_offset >= 0` would indeed be a tautological check, and it'd be checking for zero or positive.

body:
I think this topic would be more easily discussed if framed as a 2-step decision:
1. should the struct definition be understood as the declaration of the struct fields, or the definition of its memory layout?
2. if the answer is the former (struct declaration), should this PR be actually adding support for packed structs, or padded structs?

If I understand both positions correctly, @aapoalas is arguing that the answer to 1. should be the memory layout, while @Phosra understands it as the struct declaration. Am I right?
author: arnauorriols

body:
Turns out the discussion is even simpler: How does libffi actually work with structs? The answer is that it takes in the struct fields declaration, and not a memory layout definition. So my opinion is just plain unworkable from the libffi usage viewpoint.

libffi doesn't seem to offer any way to input packed structs, so I think that gets left as an exercise to the users to figure out... Maybe it would just be equal to a bunch of usizes in a struct, or such.
author: aapoalas

body: padded struct field declaration it is, then.

body:
> libffi doesn't seem to offer any way to input packed structs, so I think that gets left as an exercise to the users to figure out... Maybe it would just be equal to a bunch of usizes in a struct, or such.

I expect that it would just be giving the byte count as the amount of bytes in the struct (what the current code in question computes), treating it as a blob, and manual extraction/compression into the packed representation. (Could be risky and might break against some C compilers' layouts?)

body:
I don't think libffi is really taking ownership of the memory. The arguments are all passed as just `*mut c_void` pointers so there's no language level ownership passing happening, and the docs explicitly say:
> There is no checking that the calling convention and types in the Cif match the actual calling convention and types of fun, nor that they match the types of args.

[[1]](https://docs.rs/libffi/3.0.1/libffi/middle/struct.Cif.html#method.call)

ie. libffi does not know what this argument is a struct, it only sees a pointer and passes that along. Ownership of the memory stays with us.
author: aapoalas

body:
We create a copy of the buffer here and pass it as a parameter to `libffi` which will pass that pointer along to the libffi assembly, which will then copy the data through the pointer into the appropriate registers and stack slots as necessary.

Essentially, we're creating two copies: One for the vec and another for the actual FFI call. I think a single one should be enough so here it should be enough to just cast the pointer we get from the backing store into the `*const [u8]` pointer that we pass onto libffi as the `Arg`.

I'm not sure it makes sense to speak of a static struct...
1. It's by definition accessed through a pointer.
2. Copying or getting mutable access to a buffer / struct through a pointer is already possible using the `Deno.UnsafePointerView#getArrayBuffer()` and `Deno.UnsafePointerView#copyInto()` static methods.

The only use I can see for this is the automatic size computation but that's not hugely beneficial as the user still needs to know the internal layout of the struct to access data from the `Uint8Array` (If this returned an Array then that'd be different, but that would be a huge difference to all other related APIs.)

Hmm, we could avoid this extra copy if we allocated a Vec with the number of struct callback arguments before iterating over the arguments, and placed each struct AB we create into that Vec. The ABs would be created with just the original pointer and an empty deleter callback using the `new_backing_store_from_ptr` API.

Then, after we've called the JS callback we detach each of those ABs to make sure that JS cannot keep using the backing store whose memory is about the be released by libffi.

author: ghost

body:
Well, for one a callback that passes a stuct is kind of weird (think eg. an iterator that passes by value instead of by ref) but even weirder would be having that "passed by value", ie. owned value actually only be a reference of sorts, since it detaches at the end.

Further, at worst you might be required to call some drop FFI symbol before the callback is over to properly drop the struct. But it's sort of borrowed from the user point of view? In the end, the extra copy being needed is an unfortunate internal detail and not something that is inherent with a C callback, thus exposing that detail for what is likely a relatively minor performance improvement in a relatively cold call path does not seem like a good idea.

body:
(sorry I hadn't seen https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15118#discussion_r918421687)

It makes it so the `--reload` flag is only applied once-per-module-per-process. We previously had cases modules would be redownloaded for every dynamic import and worker if `--reload` was passed. Omitting it here would only do that for type checking which would have significantly less impact, but it's probably sound to have the once-per-module-per-process guarantee anywhere `--reload` is processed.

body:
I didn't think it was worth the extra complexity, but maybe it is? The previous tsbuildinfo cache was also unbounded.

I just wrote some tests for this and for the same entrypoint type checked multiple times with a unique change each time, the end result was this (the hashes don't take up a lot of room):

* 1,000 unique type checks - 92KB (size of sqlite db)
* 5,000 unique type checks - 316KB
* 10,000 unique type checks - 600KB

Also, I didn't notice any slowdown in speed as the database size grew.

However, once you start type checking different root entry points, then it starts to grow a lot because it stores the tsbuildinfo as well. For example, this is with unique entrypoints:

* 1,000 unique entry points - 6.1MB
* 5,000 unique entry points - ~30MB
* 10,000 unique entry points - 59.5MB

For this, I also didn't notice any slowdown as the database size grew... it remained exactly the same. SQLite seems quite fast.

I think it would be unlikely for someone to even have more than 100 root entrypoints before upgrading their CLI version. Let me know your thoughts.

body:
Is it actually safe to use a u32 as a u8 or u16 argument? I would guess that on the parameters side it would be safe, since presumably the values will be passed in the same registers even if they don't fill it all, and then reading the u32 bit register as a u8 would just mean forgetting about part of the bits there.

On return side, however, I am more worried. Returning a u8 through a register will presumably leave the unneeded bits as they were, and reading that register as a u32 will then lead to a much different number.

But I might also be wrong :)
author: aapoalas

body: This matches nomenclature from v8's fast API
author: bartlomieju

body:
Okay, I did some sleuthing into this and it seems that indeed using a smaller width return value as a larger width value is not safe. When u8 / u16 values are passed as an argument, the caller is expected to zero out the bits up to in the lower half of the register. (Actually, this might mean that even passing a u32 off as a u8 may cause a good degree of UB if the value actually overflows the u8 max limit. Users beware.)

No such guarantee (well, even the sign / zero extending of parameters is an undocumented convention that clang relies on) exists for return values, and they can contain garbage beyond their width.

So, when a native function returns a u8 containing `0b00000001`, the register might actually contain `0b11111111111111111111111100000001` and naturally reading that as a u32 (as V8 will do) will give `4294967041` instead of the expected `1`.

Some compilers might still do zero / sign bit extending on return values, but it's not a given across all systems. Also, negative sign bit extending will of course also cause the value to become quite odd indeed.

### Sources:
[1] https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40475902/is-garbage-allowed-in-high-bits-of-parameter-and-return-value-registers-in-x86-6
[2] https://stackoverflow.com/questions/36706721/is-a-sign-or-zero-extension-required-when-adding-a-32bit-offset-to-a-pointer-for/36760539#36760539
author: aapoalas

body:
Thinking on this a tad bit more:
1. For parameters, this means that the responsibility of upholding the ABI contract is transferred to the JS code, ie. essentially to whoever is using Deno FFI be that a library developer or a user of said library if raw bindings are exposed.
2. For return values, again this means that the Deno FFI user must simply expect u8 / i8 / u16 / i16 return values to potentially contain values beyond the expected value and thus they must manually zero out the top bits of any return values.
author: aapoalas

body:
> Okay, I did some sleuthing into this and it seems that indeed using a smaller width return value as a larger width value is not safe. When u8 / u16 values are passed as an argument, the caller is expected to zero out the bits up to in the lower half of the register.

Note that this particular bit of information is *ABI-dependent*, i.e., it differs per ABI, and the C ABI differs per platform. For the most part, you do not want to rely on either set of behaviours.

Furthermore, is fairly likely that an incorrect implementation here will have extension/truncation optimised out, leading to bugs [such as this Rustc bug](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/97463).

body:
I think we should extend support for 64bit `typeof arg === "number"` parameters. The Fast API supports those, and we support those in our normal FFI calls; we'll always return BigInts but we do allow users to pass in a number as a u64 / i64 / usize / isize. They're forced into only up-to 53bit values, but that's what they have in the JS numbers originally anyway so there's no risk of losing data or anything like that.

And if they need the full 64bits to represent the number, they'll use a BigInt which means that V8 will choose to not call the Fast API function.

The `can_callback` flag was introduced in a PR few days ago to allow users to opt out of the Fast API optimisation, since calls made using Fast API cannot trigger calls back into V8, or those calls will cause a panic in V8.

Presumably, the Windows builds now never see this flag used since Fast API is always disabled on Windows builds anyway, and thus this is dead code for them.

I think if it is possible, this might be better prefixed as `#[cfg(not(target_os = "windows"))]` so that Windows builds don't waste time serialising this flag for no zero use.

body:
C does not mandate that function pointers and data pointers are the same.
A quick search yielded this: <https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1473935/can-the-size-of-pointers-vary-between-data-and-function-pointers>.

See the documents that they reference: <https://c-faq.com/ptrs/generic.html>; you should use `void(*)()`, ***but*** because of C's irregular syntax regarding some types (such as functions), you should emit a type alias near the top of the file (`typedef void (*function_pointer_t)()`), and then use that here (`Function => "function_pointer_t"`).
author: ghost

body:
I opened a new issue for this: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15292

It seems that the main case where this is true is Harvard architecture, which is not supported by Deno either. These seem to usually be lower-bitness environments, eg. 16 bit data pointers, which would mean that compiling V8 for those might not be possible in the first place. Compiling Rust might not work either.
author: aapoalas

body:
> I opened a new issue for this: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15292
> 
> It seems that the main case where this is true is Harvard architecture, which is not supported by Deno either.

> These seem to usually be lower-bitness environments, eg. 16 bit data pointers, which would mean that compiling V8 for those might not be possible in the first place.

Indeed, this is the usual case, that `sizeof::<fn()>() >= size_of::<usize>()`.

> Compiling Rust might not work either.

I believe Rust could still work on such platforms, as it requests passing `fn()` as FFI functions.
<https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.fn.html>
And Rust acknowledges that function pointers may not be pointer-sized.
<https://rust-lang.github.io/unsafe-code-guidelines/layout/function-pointers.html>

But if we have confirmation that V8 can't support such platforms, then it is perfectly understandable to disregard this situation as not relevant.
(I'm not sure if V8's JiT compiler would work on such platforms, as Harvard platforms are more complicated to do so on.)

Although, another case may be CHERI and such...
author: ghost

body:
Yes, see the bottom of the PR description. It was agreed upon that breaking this was worth it in order to fix the issues.

That said, an alternative to not be breaking is to store a hash of the emit in addition to the source in the current version file. Then we would load the source, versions (two hashes), and emit then check that the emit hash matches the one in versions and the source hash matches the one in versions. If they do, then we can use the emit. By breaking here the main gain we get is that we don't need to bother hashing the emit... the more I think about it the more I would prefer this instead actually and it would be less work, though still breaking for deno_cache (because of change to versions file). Thoughts?

Edit: Oh, there is also the source map hash. That could just be moved back into the file.
